data.description:
• Considering whether Kyndryl Holdings is starting to look like value after a tough run, or if the current price still does not fully reflect the risks.

This has highlighted the challenge of reshaping contracts and streamlining operations. Approach 1: Kyndryl Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today, using a required rate of return.

For Kyndryl Holdings, the model uses a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is reported at about $414.9 million. Analyst input is provided for selected years such as 2026 to 2028, with Simply Wall St extrapolating further out to 2035. For example, projected free cash flow in 2028 is $606 million, and the later years in the 10 year schedule range from $339 million to about $1.0b of annual free cash flow, all discounted back to present value.

Combining these discounted cash flows produces an estimated intrinsic value of US$32.67 per share, compared with the recent share price of about US$12.85. On this model, the stock screens as 60.7% undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Kyndryl Holdings Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful shorthand because it links what you pay directly to the earnings the business is already generating. It also reflects what the market is willing to pay for each dollar of profit.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are. Higher growth and lower perceived risk tend to support higher P/E ratios, while slower growth or higher uncertainty often line up with lower multiples.

Kyndryl Holdings currently trades on a P/E of 11.59x. That compares with a peer average of 12.71x and an IT industry average P/E of about 19.38x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio framework suggests a P/E of 45.78x for Kyndryl Holdings, based on factors such as earnings growth characteristics, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio is designed to be more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it blends these business and risk inputs into a single figure. When set against the current 11.59x, the Fair Ratio points to Kyndryl Holdings trading below that modelled level.

data.title: NexPoint Real Estate Finance (NREF) Revenue Jump Tests Bearish Decline Narrative After FY 2025 Results
data.description:
NexPoint Real Estate Finance (NREF) just wrapped up FY 2025 with Q4 total revenue of US$30.0 million and basic EPS of US$0.67. Trailing twelve month revenue came in at US$157.3 million and EPS at US$4.28. Over the past year, the company has seen revenue move from US$73.1 million to US$157.3 million on a trailing basis, with EPS shifting from US$1.02 to US$4.28. This context allows investors to weigh these results against expectations. With margins running well ahead of the prior year, the focus now turns to how durable this profitability profile really is.

See our full analysis for NexPoint Real Estate Finance.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings story lines up with the dominant narratives around NREF, and where the data starts to challenge the usual talking points.

See what the community is saying about NexPoint Real Estate Finance

Advertisement

48.1% net margin changes the earnings mix

  • On a trailing basis, NREF earned US$75.7 million of net income on US$157.3 million of revenue, which works out to a 48.1% net profit margin compared with 24.2% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for the bullish narrative is that this high margin is coming alongside high quality earnings, with trailing earnings having grown at about 16% per year over five years and very strong growth over the last 12 months, even as analysts now expect revenue to fall around 40.5% per year and earnings to reach US$32.5 million by 2028.
    • Bulls pointing to improving fundamentals in multifamily, storage and life sciences need to square those sector drivers with forecasts that call for revenue declines of about 63.8% per year and earnings declines of about 55.5% per year over the next three years.
    • At the same time, the consensus view that margins could rise further to around 101.7% in three years sits in tension with the idea that the current 48.1% margin already benefits from unusually high loan coupons that analysts expect to normalize.

P/E of 3.3x versus industry 8.7x

  • NREF trades on a trailing P/E of 3.3x compared with about 8.7x for the US mortgage REIT industry, 6.7x for peers and 18.5x for the broader US market. The current share price of US$13.30 sits close to an analyst target of US$14.50 that implies only modest upside.
  • Critics highlight that even with this low P/E and strong trailing net income of US$75.7 million, analysts still group NREF with names that face pressure from an expected revenue decline of roughly 40.5% per year and earnings stepping down to US$32.5 million by 2028.
    • Bears argue that to justify an analyst target around US$14.50 on those 2028 earnings, the stock would need to trade at about 11.1x earnings, which is still below the current 13.1x industry P/E, so the present 3.3x multiple may reflect concern that forecasts could be revised lower.
    • What is striking is that the gap between today’s 3.3x and the implied 11.1x leaves a wide range of possible outcomes, so investors are effectively being asked to judge whether the strong trailing results or the weaker forward assumptions deserve more weight at a price only slightly under the target.
On these numbers, valuation looks cheap on trailing earnings but closely tied to analysts’ cautious revenue and EPS path by 2028, so it is worth seeing how other investors are framing that trade off in their narratives for NREF. 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about NexPoint Real Estate Finance.

15.04% dividend meets weak cash coverage

  • NREF’s dividend yield sits at 15.04%, while analysis flags that this payout is not well covered by free cash flow and that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow.
  • Bears focus on the risk that fixed obligations such as preferred equity distributions and interest costs could start to bite if the forecast revenue decline of about 63.8% per year and earnings decline of 55.5% per year materialize over the next three years.
    • Skeptics point to the rapid scaling of preferred capital at coupons around 8%, which increases fixed distributions at the same time that analysts expect revenue to move from US$157.3 million on a trailing basis to around US$31.9 million by 2028.
    • That combination, alongside the assessment that operating cash flow does not comfortably cover debt, reinforces the cautious narrative that the current 15.04% yield may rely heavily on today’s high earnings base rather than on the forecast earnings level of about US$32.5 million.

Next Steps

To see how these results tie into long-term growth, risks, and valuation, check out the full range of community narratives for NexPoint Real Estate Finance on Simply Wall St. Add the company to your watchlist or portfolio so you'll be alerted when the story evolves.

With sentiment clearly split between risks and rewards, this is the moment to look at the data yourself and decide where you stand. To get a balanced view of both sides, start with the 3 key rewards and 3 important warning signs.

See What Else Is Out There

For all the strong trailing numbers, NREF’s story leans heavily on a very high 48.1% net margin, weak cash coverage and analyst forecasts for steep revenue and earnings declines.

data.title: Syensqo (ENXTBR:SYENS) Q4 EPS Loss Deepens And Tests Bullish Profitability Narrative
data.description:
Syensqo (ENXTBR:SYENS) has wrapped up FY 2025 with fourth quarter revenue of €1,384 million and basic EPS of a €1.61 loss, while the trailing twelve months show revenue of €5,969 million and basic EPS of a €0.85 loss. Over recent quarters, revenue has moved from €1,662 million in Q4 2024 to €1,678 million in Q1 2025, €1,639 million in Q2 2025, €1,558 million in Q3 2025, and €1,384 million in Q4 2025, with EPS swinging between a €1.28 loss and a €0.49 gain before landing at a €1.61 loss this quarter. Investors are watching how these shifts translate into any potential margin recovery.

See our full analysis for Syensqo.

With the latest numbers on the table, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the prevailing narratives about Syensqo's profitability, growth potential, and risks.

See what the community is saying about Syensqo

Advertisement

Losses Persist With €87 Million TTM Net Loss

  • On a trailing twelve month basis to Q4 2025, Syensqo booked a net loss of €87 million on €5.97b of revenue, and basic EPS over that period was a loss of €0.85 per share.
  • Consensus narrative talks about revenue growing around 2.4% a year and margins moving from about a 1.5% loss to a 5.8% profit margin by 2029. However, the current €87 million loss and earlier TTM losses of €108 million and €87 million through 2025 show that profitability has not yet turned, which investors need to weigh against the expected earnings recovery.
    • Analysts reference future earnings of €374.2 million by 2029, but the latest figures still show negative net income across all TTM data points provided.
    • The forecast revenue growth rate of roughly 2.4% a year also sits below the cited 7% Belgian market reference, so the bullish margin story rests on efficiency and mix more than rapid top line expansion.

Quarterly Swings Test The Bull Case

  • Within FY 2025, net income moved from a small €3 million loss in Q1 to a €50 million profit in Q2 and €39 million in Q3, then back to a €164 million loss in Q4, showing sharp earnings volatility even as revenue stepped down more gradually from €1,678 million to €1,384 million.
  • Bulls highlight a path to profitability with earnings forecast to grow around 54.27% a year and margins rising to roughly 9.7% in three years. The heavy Q4 loss and negative TTM EPS of €0.85, however, challenge how smooth that path might be if similar quarterly swings keep showing up.
    • The bullish view expects earnings to reach €676.6 million by about 2029, yet the historical TTM data provided never shows a positive net income figure, so the turnaround would require a clear shift from what is currently reported.
    • Investors who buy into the bullish narrative are effectively betting that recent weak quarters are more temporary than the five year trend of gradually higher losses at roughly 0.5% a year.
On top of that earnings volatility, bulls and skeptics are looking at very different futures for Syensqo, so it helps to see how each side frames the same set of numbers through the 🐂 Syensqo Bull Case

Valuation Gap Versus Mixed Fundamentals

  • Syensqo trades at €49.31 per share against an analyst consensus target of €66.08 and a DCF fair value of €87.16. Its P/S multiple of 0.8x sits below the 1.0x European Chemicals average but above the 0.4x peer group level.
  • Bears point out that the company is still unprofitable on a TTM basis and that revenue growth is forecast at about 2.7% a year versus 7% for the Belgian market. They argue that the valuation gap to both the €66.08 target and €87.16 DCF fair value may be hard to close if losses and slower top line growth persist.
    • The 3.29% dividend yield is not covered by earnings, which lines up with the TTM loss of €87 million and reinforces the cautious view on cash generation.
    • Recent share price volatility over the last three months is also flagged as a key risk, making it tougher for bears to see a stable re rating while fundamentals are still catching up.
If you are weighing whether the current €49.31 price leans closer to the bullish or cautious camp, it is worth seeing how critics interpret these same earnings and valuation gaps in their full thesis via the 🐻 Syensqo Bear Case

Next Steps

With a mix of optimism and concern running through this story, it is worth checking the numbers yourself and forming a clear view, starting with the 3 key rewards and 2 important warning signs.

data.title: Charlotte's Web Holdings FY 2025 Q3 Losses Persist And Test Bullish Profitability Narratives
data.description:
Charlotte's Web Holdings (TSX:CWEB) just posted its FY 2025 third quarter numbers, with revenue of US$11.5 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.04, while the trailing twelve months show revenue of US$49.2 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.14. Over recent quarters, revenue has held in a tight band around the low US$10 million range each period and EPS has consistently reflected losses. This gives a clear picture of a business that is still spending more than it earns. For investors, the story this quarter is about how a shrinking loss profile over several years sits alongside ongoing negative EPS, which keeps the focus squarely on whether margins can improve from here.

See our full analysis for Charlotte's Web Holdings.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how these results line up against the most common narratives around Charlotte's Web Holdings and where the data starts to challenge those views.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Losses Narrow On A Trailing Basis

  • On a trailing twelve month view to FY 2025 Q3, Charlotte's Web reported US$49.2 million in revenue and a net loss of US$21.7 million, with basic EPS at a loss of US$0.14 over that period.
  • What stands out against the cautiously bullish view that the business is steadily moving toward profitability is that, while losses have been reduced at about 19.8% per year over the past five years, the latest twelve month loss of US$21.7 million still sits against fairly steady revenue around the US$49 million to US$50 million range. This means:
    • The improvement in losses over several years is happening alongside largely flat trailing revenue, so the bullish focus on a gradual earnings recovery is still tied to cost discipline rather than clear top line expansion.
    • The FY 2025 quarterly net losses of US$6.2 million, US$6.3 million and US$5.8 million in Q1, Q2 and Q3 respectively show that, even with smaller losses over time, the company remains some distance from breaking even on a run rate basis.

Quarterly Loss Pattern Still Consistent

  • Across the last six reported quarters, quarterly revenue has stayed in a relatively tight band between US$11.5 million and US$12.8 million, while quarterly net losses ranged from US$3.4 million to US$11.1 million and basic EPS losses moved between US$0.02 and US$0.07.
  • Bears often focus on the risk that an unprofitable company does not show enough operating progress, and this set of numbers partly supports that cautious view because:
    • Recent FY 2025 quarters still show EPS losses close to US$0.04 and net losses above US$5.8 million, which keeps Charlotte's Web in loss making territory despite the longer term 19.8% annual reduction in losses.
    • The fact that revenue for FY 2025 Q1 to Q3 sits close to the prior year range between US$11.5 million and US$12.8 million, without a clear shift either way, means cost control rather than revenue momentum is carrying most of the burden in the bearish risk reward discussion.

Premium P/S Multiple With Volatile Share Price

  • At a share price of US$1.02, Charlotte's Web trades on a P/S of 2.4x, compared with 0.8x for the wider Canadian pharmaceuticals industry and 1.9x for its peer group, while the share price has also been more volatile than the Canadian market over the past three months.

    • The 2.4x P/S premium over the 0.8x industry average and 1.9x peer average suggests investors are paying more for each dollar of revenue even though the trailing twelve month EPS is still a loss of US$0.14 and the company remains unprofitable.
    • When a stock with ongoing losses also shows higher recent share price volatility than the broader Canadian market, any disappointment relative to the implied expectations in that P/S premium can feed quickly into sharper short term price swings.

data.description:
JFB Construction Holdings (JFB) has just posted another tough set of numbers for FY 2025, with Q3 revenue of US$5.0 million and a basic EPS loss of US$0.05, set against a trailing 12 month revenue decline of 20.4% and no net profit over the period. The company has seen quarterly revenue move from US$7.1 million in Q4 2024 to US$5.9 million in Q1 2025, US$3.7 million in Q2 2025, and US$5.0 million in Q3 2025. EPS shifted from a modest profit of US$0.02 per share in Q4 2024 to small gains in Q1 2025, then slipped into losses of US$0.13 and US$0.05 per share in Q2 and Q3 respectively.   • Bears focus on that 20.4% revenue drop and current losses, and the quarterly pattern does give them concrete talking points:
    • Quarterly revenue moved from US$7.9 million in Q3 2024 to a range of about US$4.0 million to US$7.1 million over subsequent quarters, then to US$5.0 million in Q3 2025, which points to a smaller revenue base than a year ago.

Crypto and blockchain stocks sit at the intersection of technology, finance, and changing market plumbing, which can be appealing when geopolitical tensions, energy prices, and bond yields keep shifting. Cipher Digital (CIFR)

Overview: Cipher Digital develops and runs large scale data centers in the United States that are used primarily for Bitcoin mining today and are being adapted for high performance computing hosting, serving hyperscale tenants that need significant power and cooling capacity for AI and other compute intensive workloads.

Operations: Cipher Digital currently generates all of its US$223.9 million in revenue from Bitcoin mining operations in the United States.

Market Cap: US$5.21b

Cipher Digital is shifting from being a pure Bitcoin miner to an owner of long term AI and high performance computing infrastructure, with 15 year campus leases, a 600 MW pipeline for hyperscale tenants such as Google and AWS, and low cost power contracts that support margin potential. Analysts have highlighted the company’s revenue and earnings outlook, yet it remains unprofitable, trades at a high P/S multiple, and relies on external funding while spending heavily on new hardware and sites. Combined with share price volatility, insider selling and uncertainty around how quickly AI leases are taken up, this results in a higher risk stock that may appeal to investors who understand both the potential benefits and these pressure points.

Bitmine Immersion Technologies (BMNR)

The company also sells mining equipment and increasingly positions itself around institutional grade Ethereum staking and broader digital asset ecosystem services.

Operations: Bitmine Immersion Technologies generates US$7.19 million in revenue from the cryptocurrency mining industry, all from the United States.

Market Cap: US$9.0b

Get the full picture in the 1 key reward and 2 important warning signs (1 is major!)

Galaxy Digital (GLXY)

data.title: Is MakeMyTrip (NasdaqGS:MMYT) Pricing Fair After Recent Share Price Slide
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether MakeMyTrip at around US$37.88 is a bargain or just looks cheap, the key question is how its current price compares with its underlying worth.
  • The stock has seen sharp moves recently, with a 3.1% decline over the last 7 days, a 32.3% decline over 30 days, and year to date returns of a 53.7% decline, although the 3 year and 5 year returns sit at 61.7% and 26.6% respectively.
  • This price action comes as MakeMyTrip continues to sit in focus as a Nasdaq listed online travel player. Investors are weighing up long term demand for digital travel bookings against changing risk appetite in growth oriented names. Broader sentiment around US listed consumer services companies has also shaped how quickly investors are willing to reprice expectations.
  • Right now, MakeMyTrip holds a value score of 1 out of 6. This raises useful questions about how different valuation checks, from cash flow models to multiples, compare with each other and how a more complete way of thinking about valuation comes together at the end of this article.

MakeMyTrip scores just 1/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: MakeMyTrip Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company might be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today using a required rate of return.

For MakeMyTrip, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The company’s latest twelve month free cash flow is about US$208 million. Analyst inputs and subsequent extrapolations by Simply Wall St feed into ten year projections, with free cash flow for 2035 estimated at around US$492 million.

Rolling all of these projected cash flows together, the DCF model arrives at an intrinsic value of about US$40.75 per share. Compared with the recent share price of roughly US$37.88, this implies the stock is trading at around a 7.1% discount to this estimate, which sits in the “close but slightly cheap” zone rather than a clear-cut bargain.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

MakeMyTrip is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for MakeMyTrip.

Approach 2: MakeMyTrip Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about what you are paying for each dollar of current earnings. It links directly to the bottom line, which is usually where equity investors focus first.

What counts as a "normal" P/E depends a lot on how quickly earnings are expected to grow and how risky those earnings are. Higher growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk often calls for a lower one.

MakeMyTrip currently trades on a P/E of 63.32x. That sits above the Hospitality industry average of 20.62x and also above the broader peer average of 24.87x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for MakeMyTrip is 52.19x, which reflects a preferred P/E that factors in elements such as earnings growth, profit margin, market cap, industry and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the industry, because it adjusts for the company’s own profile rather than assuming that all Hospitality names deserve the same multiple. With the current P/E at 63.32x versus a Fair Ratio of 52.19x, the shares screen as OVERVALUED on this metric.

Result: OVERVALUED

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Narratives are Simply Wall St’s way for you to attach a clear story to the numbers by writing out your view on MakeMyTrip, linking that story to assumptions on future revenue, earnings and margins, and then turning it into a fair value that you can compare with the current price on the Community page used by millions of investors. A very optimistic investor might lean toward the higher analyst target of US$117.0, while a more cautious investor might anchor on US$85.0.
